type="submit">Sign Up </button> </div> <div class="Form-items-item"> <input type="hidden" name="acquisition_campaign" value="ARTS_Affordance"> </div> <div class="Form-newsletter-disclaimer"> <p>By subscribing, you agree with WBEZ’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/terms-of-use" target="_blank" >Terms of Use</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/privacy-policy" target="_blank" >Privacy Policy</a></p> </div> </form> </bsp-form> </div> </div><p>A steady decline in drinking is partially to blame. The U.S. drinking rate reached historic lows in 2025, according to <a class="Link" href="https://news.gallup.com/poll/693362/drinking-rate-new-low-alcohol-concerns-surge.aspx" target="_blank" ><u>data from Gallup</u></a>. But the recent string of local closings was accelerated by rising operational and production costs, including aluminum tariffs, and what many feel is an oversaturated market, owners said.</p><p>The rash of announcements has surviving brewers on guard, and several said they are looking at everything from better-than-average food to live events to stay afloat in 2026 and beyond.</p><p>“The breweries that have survived are the ones that pride themselves on consistency and people knowing what they're going to get when they purchase that beer,” said Trevor Rose-Hamblin, a longtime brewer and co-founder of Old Irving Brewing.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-6a0000" name="image-6a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/43e49d8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F90%2F02%2Fb6ac519f44d99771d1bbf2e2a833%2Fgrid-oldirving-oib.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/873ecb1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F90%2F02%2Fb6ac519f44d99771d1bbf2e2a833%2Fgrid-oldirving-oib.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“We all kind of know that the beer industry or liquor industry has kind of slowed down,” Trevor Rose-Hamblin said, pictured here in 2019. “Being able to have food and a place for people to dine and a more convenient location are all things that we have that really help.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Brian Rich/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Besides game and trivia nights, concerts and other concerted efforts to get people in the door, breweries are raising their prices to match inflation and trying to market to younger and more health-conscious consumers with alternative products, such as THC- and CBD-infused beverages.</p><p>Despite their efforts, brewery closures in Chicago mirror what’s happening in the rest of the country. According to annual reports <a class="Link" href="https://www.brewersassociation.org/association-news/the-2025-year-in-beer/" target="_blank" ><u>from the Brewers Association</u></a>, national brewery closings outpaced openings in 2024 and 2025 — the steepest decline since 2020, when the COVID-19 pandemic began.</p><p>But that’s only about a 1% net decrease in breweries across the U.S., said Matt Gacoich, staff economist at the Brewers Association.</p><p>“There will certainly be more closings. There will also be more openings,” he said. “After a certain point, just like any industry, you reach the point of market saturation. There were indications that craft beer was getting to that point prior to the pandemic.”</p><h3><b>The elusive N/A market</b></h3><p>Chicago saw craft beer boom a little more than a decade ago. Since 2011, the <a class="Link" href="https://www.brewersassociation.org/statistics-and-data/state-craft-beer-stats/" target="_blank" ><u>number of breweries in Illinois</u></a> climbed from 54 to nearly 300 in 2024, but the existing amount has mostly plateaued since the pandemic year, according to the Brewers Association’s report.</p><p>“There's just been a race to the bottom really since about 2020,” said Brian Buckman, founder and head brewer at Illuminated Brew Works, which is closing at the end of June.</p><p>Delayed by COVID-19, Illuminated opened its taproom in 2021. The founders signed the lease two years prior, he said.“[We] just kept seeing the industry constrict,” Buckman said.</p><p>One thing brewers tried to do is market to guests<a class="Link" href="https://www.brewersassociation.org/insights/the-state-of-non-alc/" target="_blank" ><u> who don’t drink alcohol</u></a>. But pivoting to meet that demand has not been as easy as it might seem. Vacuum distillation, one of the processes used to remove alcohol from beer, can require expensive equipment, ingredients, tests and time.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-410000" name="image-410000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e8ee7a0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F4f%2F893be8b44736976e13a8b6df3f4f%2Fbeercrash-260315-2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/40af54a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F4f%2F893be8b44736976e13a8b6df3f4f%2Fbeercrash-260315-2.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/42e6194/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F4f%2F893be8b44736976e13a8b6df3f4f%2Fbeercrash-260315-2.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Brian Taylor, brewmaster and owner of Winer Beer Company stands in the barroom of the former Whiner Beer Company and the future SomosMonos Taproom located at The Plant in Back of the Yards neighborhood, Friday, March 6, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/42e6194/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F4f%2F893be8b44736976e13a8b6df3f4f%2Fbeercrash-260315-2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/461f4ee/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F4f%2F893be8b44736976e13a8b6df3f4f%2Fbeercrash-260315-2.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/42e6194/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F4f%2F893be8b44736976e13a8b6df3f4f%2Fbeercrash-260315-2.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“We definitely tried to pivot, while keeping obviously beer as our main source of income,” Taylor said.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Whiner Beer Company opened in 2016 inside a food business incubator called The Plant (1400 W. 46th St.) in Back of the Yards. But two months shy of its 10th anniversary, the brewery will close.</p><p>Co-owner Brian Taylor said that Whiner has attempted to break into the non-alcoholic and low-ABV market with sparkling teas and yerba mate drinks.</p><p>“We definitely tried to pivot, while keeping obviously beer as our main source of income,” Taylor said. “We still do pretty well with [those]… but nothing that would keep the doors open.”</p><p>Whiner’s best-selling beer is the Le Tub, a refreshing barrel-aged saison or Belgian-style farmhouse ale. Their last spring edition variant is a watermelon-flavored kettle-soured brew known as Bubble Tub.</p><p>Some breweries took inspiration from the “California sober” movement and the flood of THC dispensaries after Illinois legalized marijuana for commercial consumption in 2020, opening the door for THC-infused drinks. But even as products with hemp-derived THC are becoming increasingly popular, the industry is still largely unregulated.</p><p>That has made it an unstable option. Gov. J.B. Pritzker has threatened a ban in Illinois, and the Chicago City Council nearly banned hemp-derived THC in January. (The city ultimately <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/01/21/chicago-hemp-ban-thc-beverages-topical-marty-quinn-brandon-johnson" target="_blank" ><u>created a loophole</u></a> by allowing exceptions for beverages and ointments).</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div style="background-color: #F4F4F4; border-radius: 50%; height: 12.5px; width: 12.5px; transform: translateX(0px) translateY(7px);"></div> <div style="background-color: #F4F4F4; height: 12.5px; transform: rotate(-45deg) translateX(3px) translateY(1px); width: 12.5px; flex-grow: 0; margin-right: 14px; margin-left: 2px;"></div> <div style="background-color: #F4F4F4; border-radius: 50%; height: 12.5px; width: 12.5px; transform: translateX(9px) translateY(-18px);"></div></div><div style="margin-left: 8px;"> <div style=" background-color: #F4F4F4; border-radius: 50%; flex-grow: 0; height: 20px; width: 20px;"></div> <div style=" width: 0; height: 0; border-top: 2px solid transparent; border-left: 6px solid #f4f4f4; border-bottom: 2px solid transparent; transform: translateX(16px) translateY(-4px) rotate(30deg)"></div></div><div style="margin-left: auto;"> <div style=" width: 0px; border-top: 8px solid #F4F4F4; border-right: 8px solid transparent; transform: translateY(16px);"></div> <div style=" background-color: #F4F4F4; flex-grow: 0; height: 12px; width: 16px; transform: translateY(-4px);"></div> <div style=" width: 0; height: 0; border-top: 8px solid #F4F4F4; border-left: 8px solid transparent; transform: translateY(-4px) translateX(8px);"></div></div></div> <div style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; flex-grow: 1; justify-content: center; margin-bottom: 24px;"> <div style=" background-color: #F4F4F4; border-radius: 4px; flex-grow: 0; height: 14px; margin-bottom: 6px; width: 224px;"></div> <div style=" background-color: #F4F4F4; border-radius: 4px; flex-grow: 0; height: 14px; width: 144px;"></div></div></a></div></blockquote> <script async src="//platform.instagram.com/en_US/embeds.js"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>For some brewers, the risk is just too great, said Alarmist’s owner and founder Gary Gulley.</p><p>“That’s a [legal] gray area,” he said. “Every brewery is going to try to make that same product. So, you're going to be right back where you started trying to differentiate yourself.</p><p>”COVID-19 also left some brewers with a financial hangover. Owners still had to pay for buildings, equipment and production. Gulley made the difficult decision to close to avoid filing for bankruptcy.</p><p>“We had to shut the taproom down, and if you don't have a taproom, you don't have a brewery,” Gulley said. “I assumed, as did a lot of people, that we'd come through it and people would start coming back. … But for a lot of us, we took on more debt just to get us through COVID.”</p><p>Grain, hops and other materials have also increased in price over the years: Illuminated’s Buckman cited a nearly 30% year-over-year increase since 2021. President Donald Trump’s added layer of tariffs on imported goods doubled the price of aluminum that many brewers purchase to can products.</p><p>In 2017, Whiner purchased cans from a Canadian company at nine cents per can, but the price is now approximately 16 cents per can.</p><p>“All this does is put more pressure on us to raise prices, and that just scares people away. It's like this double-edged sword,” Whiner’s Taylor said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-990000" name="image-990000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/961535a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F50%2F9e%2F8deaa36b44118546cd0c513ab257%2Fgrannyevents-062325-17.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1029961/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F50%2F9e%2F8deaa36b44118546cd0c513ab257%2Fgrannyevents-062325-17.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c0266b4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F50%2F9e%2F8deaa36b44118546cd0c513ab257%2Fgrannyevents-062325-17.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Jessica Woodburn teaches a class on how to make needle felted succulents at Old Irving Brewing Co., at 4419 W. Montrose Ave., on Tuesday, June 17, 2025." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c0266b4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F50%2F9e%2F8deaa36b44118546cd0c513ab257%2Fgrannyevents-062325-17.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7d00f9c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F50%2F9e%2F8deaa36b44118546cd0c513ab257%2Fgrannyevents-062325-17.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c0266b4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F50%2F9e%2F8deaa36b44118546cd0c513ab257%2Fgrannyevents-062325-17.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Breweries have diversified their entertainment options in recent years, expanding past trivia nights and concerts. Here, Jessica Woodburn teaches a class on how to make needle-felted succulents at Old Irving Brewing Co.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Zubaer Khan/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Suds have to stay nimble</b></h3><p>Chicago is still, and likely always will be, a beer city. Some of the city’s surviving breweries say they are trying everything they can to stay afloat, including raising prices.</p><p>Raising prices has become inevitable, but cutting corners is not, said Paul Kreiner, Noon Whistle’s head brewer.</p><p>“We've bit the bullet with some of these things and we're just making smaller margins, unfortunately,” Kreiner said. “But we do have to stay competitive with the big guys, and also we have to stay true to ourselves and make sure that we are making the best quality beer possible.”</p><p>But in an inflationary era, is a price bump enough?</p><p>Noon Whistle stays, in Kreiner’s words, “nimble” and “ahead of the times,” paying close attention to trends, such as beers that incorporate guava and mango flavors, and by serving food at their Lombard brewpub.</p><p>The brewery has gone out of its way to lean into audience-grabbing measures, such as a Cicada-infused Malört. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/environment/cicada-watch-2024/2024/05/21/cicada-infused-shots-served-at-lombard-bar-noon-whistle-pub" target="_blank" ><u>Silly and creative drinks</u></a> get people in the door, or at the very least, talking about the brand.</p><p>But the secret sauce is definitely two-fold, Kreiner said: “We're trying to stay ahead of those curves,” he said, “but also what are people going back to and drinking and consistently buying?”<b> </b>For Noon Whistle, that’s the year-round best-selling citrusy hazy IPA known as <a class="Link" href="https://www.noonwhistlebrewing.com/beers/year-round-beers/" target="_blank" ><u>Gummylicious</u></a>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> alt="From left, co-founders of SomosMonos, Rocío and Victor Santoyo, and René Lemus stand together at the bar of the former Whiner Beer Company and the future SomosMonos Taproom located at The Plant in Back of the Yards neighborhood, Friday, March 6, 2026." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4b74424/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc7%2F6d%2Fc8ff968e44ed817d1073aba96c67%2Fbeercrash-260315-16.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c5e36c3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc7%2F6d%2Fc8ff968e44ed817d1073aba96c67%2Fbeercrash-260315-16.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4b74424/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc7%2F6d%2Fc8ff968e44ed817d1073aba96c67%2Fbeercrash-260315-16.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>While Whiner Beer Company is winding down operations at The Plant in Back of the Yards, SomosMonos will scale up operations. Husband and wife duo Rocío and Victor Santoyo (left, center), along with their longtime friend Rene Lemus (right), will take over the lease and all the brewing equipment that comes with it.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“We all kind of know that the beer industry or liquor industry has kind of slowed down,” Old Irving’s Rose-Hamblin said. “Being able to have food and a place for people to dine and a more convenient location are all things that we have that really help.”</p><p>And a brewery’s ability to create meaningful spaces for people to connect, especially in a post-lockdown world, is what people are seeking more of in 2026, <a class="Link" href="https://www.brewersassociation.org/association-news/the-2025-year-in-beer/" target="_blank" ><u>according to consumer research</u></a>. Customers at Old Irving Brewing enjoy trivia nights and games.</p><p>And because there will always be a natural churn in the market, as one closes, another expands. In 2024, Whiner Beer Co. invited SomosMonos Cervercería to serve its product alongside their own in a shared taproom called The Jungle.</p><p>Now, as Whiner exits, SomosMonos will scale up operations at The Plant. Husband and wife duo Rocío and Victor Santoyo, along with their longtime friend Rene Lemus, will take over the lease and all the brewing equipment that comes with it.</p><p>The trio — who have been participating in beer festivals and beer pop-ups for years — have built music into their brewery plans. Because Victor and Lemus met as teenagers and eventually formed a band, they have a shared desire for a space where musical artists from the South Side can perform.</p><p>Now, they’re hosting events featuring local musical acts like <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/braidedjanes/" target="_blank" ><u>Braided Janes</u></a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/fuegodecumbiaband/" target="_blank" ><u>Fuego de Cumbia</u></a> every weekend.Their taproom is currently open Thursday to Sunday, but the team is open to expanding hours in the future.“Now that we have this opportunity, we cannot just let it go,” Lemus said.</p>
entries.author: Ambar Colón

entries.title: Capturing famed Chatham cleaners is a point of pride for some artists
entries.summary: <p>Chicago visual artist Brandon Breaux was in New York City this week when he heard Pride Cleaners, a Chatham architectural icon and the neighborhood dry cleaners of his youth, closed late last month.</p><p>"That makes you think, 'What is going to happen to this building?'" Breaux, 43, said. "It looks like a UFO. When they designed this building, it was an ambitious thing. The roof pitch is crazy. Everything about it is inspired."</p><p>And inspirational, for some artists.</p><p>In 2022, Breaux created a well-received and visually powerful work depicting the daringly modernist 1959 dry cleaners at 79th Street and St. Lawrence Avenue, showing its slanted roof and multicolored exterior electric sign.</p><p>The word "Dignity" rises behind the building like the sun.</p><p>"I've always been inspired by the building and the sign," Breaux said. "It tells the South Side story."</p><p>Take a quick trip through social media and you'll see a host of visual artists, photographers and others whose work showcases the building.</p><p>And no wonder. Pride Cleaners represents the kind of attention-grabbing roadside modernism that can be seen in places like Palm Springs, California, and not often in broad shouldered Chicago.</p><p>A building like that is bound to fire-up an artist's imagination.</p><p>Chicago graphic artist and illustrator Steve Shanabruch has also captured the cleaners. He even created an entire typeface based on the seven different mod letters on the Pride Cleaners' sign.</p><p>"The combination of the sign and the building" is attractive, Shanabruch said. "It's like — amazing."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ed0000" name="image-ed0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c5fd4c5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3602x2022+0+789/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F47%2F23%2Feffa8c2b49008844298d33c413b9%2Fimg-9306.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Graphic artist Steve Shanabruch imagined a new font based on Pride Cleaners’ sign.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided by Steve Shanabruch</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Design that inspires</h3><p>Breaux said he discovered the cleaners while growing up in the Chatham area. The hard-to-miss building helped him find his way around the neighborhood.</p><p>He began doing paintings of the building in 2016, when he was invited to create an exhibition of his work.</p><p>"I was struggling with these ideas of using notable landmarks and notable sculptures like The Bean," Breaux said. "But what I really relied on was my perspective of Chicago from my vantage point. And Pride Cleaners was one of the first pieces I did."</p><p>Don Hammontree, who lives in Salem, Massachusetts, but spent the 1990s in Chicago, painted Pride Cleaners in ink and bright acrylics after reading a book that featured the building.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-7e0000" name="image-7e0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Don Hammontree, a former Chicagoan, painted Pride Cleaners out of a fondness for “futuristic” architecture.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided by Don Hammontree</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>"I’ve taken a liking to futuristic architecture over the years, particularly mid-century modern," he said. "And between the colorful signage and the general look of the place, I decided to do a painting of it."</p><p>Meanwhile, the font Shanabruch derived from the Pride Cleaners sign is a clever tribute to another design aspect of the building.</p><p>"It's pretty geometric," he said of the sign's letters. "You could pretty much guess what other letters will look like just based off of the letters that are included there. It was a fun challenge for me."</p><h3>‘I hope it stays’</h3><p>Built in 1959 and designed by Chicago architect Gerald Siegwart, Pride Cleaners <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/architecture-design/2026/02/26/pride-dry-cleaners-modernist-79th-street-chatham-film-ali-chance-rapper-video" target="_blank" >closed on Feb. 28</a> after its newest owners shut down the business following just seven months of ownership.</p><p>The building's future is uncertain. But if there's any building in Chicago — any building at all — that cries out for reuse and protected landmark status, it's this one.</p><p>That includes the sign, which must be restored and remain right there in the parking lot along 79th Street.</p><p>Anything less would be an utter disservice to this historic building and the neighborhood and city in which it sits.</p><p>"I hope it stays there," Breaux said. "And it gets reused in some way that allows it to be there."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" entries.author: Lee Bey

entries.title: VA drops appeal in Supreme Court case, giving another 1.2 million vets extra education benefits
entries.summary: <p>The U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs has withdrawn from a court battle related to the landmark Supreme Court ruling in favor of Army veteran James Rudisill, creating a path for 1.2 million veterans who had no break in service to access up to 12 additional months of college through their GI Bill benefits.</p><p>The VA said in a Feb. 27 email to some beneficiaries that it's "making it easier for eligible Veterans to receive their additional G.I. Bill benefits," as the result of the Rudisill ruling and a related decision by the U.S. Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims. The VA said it "will automatically evaluate your file and inform you if additional action is required."</p><p>Since the 2024 <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/gibill/2024/04/16/gibill-rudisill-gi-bill-va-supreme-court-veterans-benefits-decorated-army-vet" >Supreme Court decision</a>, the VA had been excluding veterans who had one continuous period of service from accessing the maximum 48 months of benefits under both the Montgomery and Post-9/11 GI Bills. Instead, the department was applying the court's decision to vets who, like Rudisill, had a break in their service.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/gibill/2024/04/16/gibill-rudisill-gi-bill-va-supreme-court-veterans-benefits-decorated-army-vet" >Supreme Court ruling in GI Bill case could force VA to give more than 1 million vets greater college benefits</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Their narrow interpretation was challenged in a second case, and in May, the U.S. Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims ruled it was wrong to exclude vets who'd served continuously — the ruling the VA has now decided not to appeal.</p><p>Timothy McHugh, one of several dozen attorneys around the country who worked pro bono, said it's a relief the VA dropped its stance.</p><p>"This is a huge step in the right direction," McHugh, a former Army paratrooper and Virgina attorney with Troutman Pepper Locke, said.</p><p>But McHugh and other attorneys said the fight isn't over.</p><p>Now, there's another legal case — a rulemaking challenge — in the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit in Washington to try to straighten out what the veterans' attorneys say is contradictory language in the rules the VA uses to evaluate claims. The challenge is supported by the Veterans of Foreign Wars, Iraq and Afghanistan Veterans of America and the Commonwealth of Virginia.</p><p>"The old rules are still on the books," attorney Mary Grace Metcalfe, of Troutman Pepper Locke, said. Metcalfe called the VA's turnabout a positive "first step."</p><p>Attorney David DePippo, a Coast Guard veteran who works for Dominion Energy and has spent years volunteering on the Rudisill case, said he hopes veterans get what they earned.</p><p>Otherwise, "this is just another way to deny what [the VA] never thought was appropriate in the first place," DePippo said.</p><p>The long-running court fights could affect 2.2 million or more veterans who earned college benefits under both the Montgomery and Post-9/11 <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/gi-bill" >GI Bills</a>.</p><p>Rudisill began his court fight in 2015, after he was denied the ability to fully use both his GI Bill plans. He argued <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/11/8/23952338/supreme-court-rudisill-mcdonough-army-veterans-affairs-post911-gi-bill-montgomery-college-tuition" >the VA shortchanged him</a> out of 12 months of payments — money he was counting on to attend Yale University's divinity school.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-780000" name="image-780000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7731f40/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4509x2531+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Feb%2F56%2F9a53a32a4f4fbac79b8fa7c719bc%2Fwatchdogs.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/15acaf1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4509x2531+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Feb%2F56%2F9a53a32a4f4fbac79b8fa7c719bc%2Fwatchdogs.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d2b0324/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4509x2531+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Feb%2F56%2F9a53a32a4f4fbac79b8fa7c719bc%2Fwatchdogs.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="James Rudisill, FBI agent and retired Army captain, sits on a couch at his home in Virginia. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d2b0324/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4509x2531+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Feb%2F56%2F9a53a32a4f4fbac79b8fa7c719bc%2Fwatchdogs.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/76f68af/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4509x2531+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Feb%2F56%2F9a53a32a4f4fbac79b8fa7c719bc%2Fwatchdogs.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d2b0324/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4509x2531+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Feb%2F56%2F9a53a32a4f4fbac79b8fa7c719bc%2Fwatchdogs.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>James Rudisill, FBI agent and retired Army captain, at his home in Virginia. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Julia Rendleman/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The Supreme Court ruled 7-2 in April 2024 that Rudisill should've been allowed to tap both plans for up to a maximum of 48 months, as Congress intended.</p><p>Kassidy Perkins, of Virginia, served continuously in the Air Force for six years and was one of the plaintiffs in the Rudisill-related case. Perkins said many veterans like her are counting on the extra 12 months of benefits for graduate or law school.</p><p>"I do think fair's fair. We were told something at basic training and then to be told six years later that that was not the case — it's certainly not an uncommon experience for veterans to feel that they were shortchanged on benefits," Perkins said.</p><p>VA press secretary Pete Kasperowicz said in an email that "after the court decision, VA worked for months with both political and career VA staff to develop the training, policies and procedures needed to appropriately implement the ruling." </p><p>The VA didn't comment on the rulemaking fight. Another question left unanswered is whether the VA will reimburse veterans who took out loans to pay for school while the court battles dragged on.</p><p>Grant Jacobson, deputy superintendent of the Veterans Assistance Commission of Cook County said the <a class="Link" href="https://chicagolandveteranbusinessowners.org/veterans-assistance-commission-of-cook-county/?gad_source=1&gad_campaignid=23317304769&gbraid=0AAAABAmEXI7mRYZ1e8Ju96Ecx48VSp1tZ&gclid=Cj0KCQjwgr_NBhDFARIsAHiUWr7kp95hV9-EwWQGBJmPRNC8q1ICmq45X5lFB6L8mUrPTXKjO0boa4AaAopFEALw_wcB" target="_blank" >office</a> is following the Rudisill litigation closely so it can advise veterans. The office also administers emergency financial assistance to eligible veterans and helps connect them to benefits they've earned, Jacobson said.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/08/20/veterans-sue-va-rudisill-education-benefits-post911-gi-bill" >In GI Bill case fallout, veterans sue VA over denied education benefits</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/03/20/illinois-ag-raoul-veterans-army-air-force-gibill-benefits-rudisill-supreme-court" >Illinois backs veterans claiming they were denied GI Bill benefits despite Supreme Court ruling</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/01/07/gibill-rudisill-veterans-benefits-supreme-court-decorated-army-vet" >VA adds GI Bill benefits for more than 1 million veterans after Supreme Court ruling for decorated vet</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Stephanie Zimmermann

entries.title: Why aren’t there wind turbines in Lake Michigan?
entries.summary: <p><i>This coverage is made possible through a partnership between WBEZ and </i><a class="Link" href="http://grist.org/" target="_blank" ><i>Grist</i></a><i>, a nonprofit environmental media organization.</i></p><p>For years, state Rep. Marcus Evans Jr. has championed a plan to make Illinois’ shores home to the first offshore wind farm in the Great Lakes. But the effort to bring wind power to Lake Michigan stalled in Springfield. Again.</p><p>“I did not refile the bill in this general assembly because I didn’t have the support,” said Evans, a South Side Chicago Democrat. “But I will file in the future.”</p><p>The legislation, known as <a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/legislation/BillStatus?GAID=18&DocNum=1442&DocTypeID=SB&LegId=159982&SessionID=114" target="_blank" ><u>the Illinois Rust Belt to Green Belt Pilot Program Act</u></a>, was designed to direct state planners to solicit proposals for a new utility-scale offshore wind farm and begin purchasing offshore wind power. First <a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/documents/legislation/102/HB/PDF/10200HB4543lv.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>introduced in 2022</u></a>, the measure has long failed to pick up enough momentum to break out of the statehouse. In 2025, Illinois Sen. Robert Peters <a class="Link" href="https://ilga.gov/Legislation/BillStatus?DocNum=2132&GAID=17&GA=103&DocTypeID=HB&LegID=145649&SessionID=112" target="_blank" ><u>refiled the bill</u></a>, but it still awaits a committee assignment.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-floatRight> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-090000" name="image-090000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d230785/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9b%2Fec%2Feac089174c168da0f8ca2e3b0434%2Fccwindenergy-260311-13.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>State Rep. Marcus Evans (IL-33rd) at Steelworkers park in South Chicago, Wednesday, March 4, 2026. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Offshore wind farms are rare. There are only three operational projects in the entire U.S. and several more under construction, all on the East Coast.</p><p>To date, there are no wind turbines of the Great Lakes, much less Lake Michigan — though it isn’t for lack of wind. The wind that sweeps across the lakes is more consistent, stronger and less turbulent than its land-based counterpart, which could translate to significant carbon-free electrical generation. So what’s the holdup?</p><p>Every attempt to tap the wind power of the world’s largest freshwater ecosystem has failed, despite major potential to decarbonize the region. Without state, regional and federal policies to support the burgeoning industry, offshore wind farms in the region remain unlikely. In 2023, the National Laboratory of the Rockies estimated the Great Lakes states have enough offshore wind potential to generate more than three times more of their combined annual electrical consumption.</p><p>“If it’s done correctly and we’re able to harness even a fraction of that, we could offset a lot of electricity demand,” said Melissa Scanlan, director of the Center for Water Policy at the University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee. “It could also create conditions where some states are in the position of exporting carbon free electricity."</p><p>On paper, Scanlan said, the Great Lakes are primed for offshore wind development. Unlike the ocean coasts, where the federal government controls what happens on the seabed, Great Lakes states have jurisdiction over what happens on their shores. While projects would still need federal permits, Scanlan said this gives states a potential policy lever to meet their clean energy targets. For example, Illinois passed the <a class="Link" href="https://www.citizensutilityboard.org/climate-and-equitable-jobs-act/" target="_blank" ><u>Climate and Equitable Jobs Act</u></a> in 2021, which committed the state to transition its energy sector to 100 percent clean energy by 2050, according to the Citizens Utility Board, a utility watchdog organization. Michigan, Minnesota and Wisconsin have all set similar benchmarks.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f00000" name="image-f00000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6d9f5c2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+500/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fad%2F1c%2F6303b9f946daaf101bd39aa67d7a%2Fi002289.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9a63038/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+500/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fad%2F1c%2F6303b9f946daaf101bd39aa67d7a%2Fi002289.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7990a43/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+500/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fad%2F1c%2F6303b9f946daaf101bd39aa67d7a%2Fi002289.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="world's fair windmills" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7990a43/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+500/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fad%2F1c%2F6303b9f946daaf101bd39aa67d7a%2Fi002289.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c4fdfa4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+500/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fad%2F1c%2F6303b9f946daaf101bd39aa67d7a%2Fi002289.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7990a43/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+500/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fad%2F1c%2F6303b9f946daaf101bd39aa67d7a%2Fi002289.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Wind energy has a long history in the region. The windmill exhibit was on display at the World’s Columbian Exposition, Chicago, Illinois, 1893.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chicago History Museum, ICHi-002289</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Whether offshore wind farms ever become an answer to the region’s impending clean energy deadline hinges largely on local officials, according to Scanlan.</p><p>“They can if they choose to. If they choose not to, then nothing happens,” Scanlan said. “That brings us to today, because there are no wind turbines permitted on any of the Great Lakes currently.”</p><p>More than a decade ago, enthusiasm after President Barack Obama’s push for renewable energy buoyed a short-lived boom in potential offshore wind projects and planning in the Great Lakes. The initial boost only propelled one project forward: Ohio’s Icebreaker Wind in Lake Erie. However, that project called it quits in 2023 after facing regulatory road bumps, litigation and eventually running out of money.</p><p>Now offshore wind developers face an increasingly hostile federal landscape. On his first day back in office, President Donald Trump signed a memorandum shutting down all offshore wind work permitting approvals in the United States, effectively suspending five large-scale wind projects on the East Coast.</p><p>“That told us that this was not the time to reintroduce floating offshore wind in Illinois,” said Jim Lanard, co-founder of Magellan Wind, a floating offshore wind company focused on the Great Lakes. “Rather, we needed to face the existential threat to the industry.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c20000" name="image-c20000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7802985/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2032x1140+0+45/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0f%2F7e%2F8efb4dac4dfba3775b3483f54189%2Fcape-wind.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b9cb48a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2032x1140+0+45/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0f%2F7e%2F8efb4dac4dfba3775b3483f54189%2Fcape-wind.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1a7d97a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2032x1140+0+45/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0f%2F7e%2F8efb4dac4dfba3775b3483f54189%2Fcape-wind.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Cape Wind" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1a7d97a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2032x1140+0+45/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0f%2F7e%2F8efb4dac4dfba3775b3483f54189%2Fcape-wind.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cbe2c20/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2032x1140+0+45/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0f%2F7e%2F8efb4dac4dfba3775b3483f54189%2Fcape-wind.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1a7d97a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2032x1140+0+45/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0f%2F7e%2F8efb4dac4dfba3775b3483f54189%2Fcape-wind.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>In this July 11, 2006 file photo, a vessel sails towards a wind farm off the coast of Whitstable on the north Kent coast in southeastern England. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>David Bebber/AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Court orders have recently reversed some of Trump’s actions. But Lanard said the regulatory uncertainty means it’s a bad time for business. His company has temporarily paused its work on the Great Lakes.</p><p>“Governors and legislatures are so focused on trying to figure out how to run their governments with the reduced support coming from Washington, D.C.,” Lanard said. “It’s not time for them to add additional burdens onto their staff, who don’t know how they’re going to balance their budgets.”</p><p>Though Illinois’ jurisdiction over part of Lake Michigan presents some opportunities to work around Trump, Lanard said developers are not going to come to Illinois until there is a federal policy to protect the industry from whiplash elections. Moreover, Illinois and the other Great Lakes states have yet to establish a substantial legal framework to make local offshore wind farms a reality.</p><p>Lanard estimates it will be some time before there are any wind farms on the Great Lakes.</p><p>“I’m looking at five to seven years from 2029,” Lanard said.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-870000" name="module-870000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3><b>More about our question-asker</b></h3><br>Lisa Braganca is a lawyer living in Wilmette, though she considers herself a Hyde Parker at heart. On a recent drive back to Chicago from Nashville, she passed by fields of wind turbines. She thought they looked beautiful and useful. It got her wondering.<br><br>“We’re the Windy City,” she said. “How come we don’t have lots of windmills and wind turbines in our very windy Lake Michigan?”<br><br>Braganca said turbines could be a good option to make Illinois less dependent on fossil fuels. She added that if she had a big enough yard, she would check if she could power her home with her own wind turbine.<br><br>“I happen to think the giant windmills look nice,” Branganca said. “I take pictures of them when I am driving through downstate Illinois — obviously only when I am in the passenger seat, never when I am driving.”<br><br></div> </div><p></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-750000" name="html-embed-module-750000"></a><script async src="https://modules.wearehearken.com/wbez/embed/4190.js"></script> </div> </div><p></p>

entries.title: CTA bulks up safety measures to keep federal funding
entries.summary: <h3>Good morning, Chicago. ✶</h3><p>🔎 <b>Below</b>: Chicago fends off federal funding cuts to public transit with a beefed-up new security plan.</p><p>🗞️ <b>Plus</b>: Mayor Brandon Johnson seeks physical protection at Chicago voting locations in echo of anti-immigration enforcement ordinances, an exhibit of Oscar-winning costumes at the Griffin MSI and more news you need to know.</p><p>📝 <b>Keeping score</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtljdil-tlkjhhnv-j/" target="_blank" >Northwestern Wildcats beat Penn State</a>, 76-66, in the Big Ten Tournament opener.</p><p>🕛: An 8-minute read</p><hr><p></p><h3>TODAY’S WEATHER 🌧️</h3><p>Rain and some snow with a high near 39.</p><hr><p></p><h3>TODAY’S TOP STORY 🔎</h3><div class="Enhancement" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Federal Transit Administration criticized the Chicago Transit Authority’s earlier security plan unveiled in December as “materially deficient.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtljdil-tlkjhhnv-i/" target="_blank" >CTA’s new security plan includes sheriff’s deputies on trains, high-barrier gates</a></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtljdil-tlkjhhnv-d/" target="_blank" >David Struett</a></h5><p><b>Safety first</b>: The Chicago Transit Authority is deploying sheriff’s deputies on its trains, installing high-barrier entry gates to deter fare evasion and conducting "farecard inspection missions," all in response to the CTA's federal funding being threatened.</p><p><b>Federal oversight</b>: The CTA presented those changes in a new security plan Tuesday to the Federal Transit Administration, which had been threatening to a cut a $50 million grant if the CTA did not revise its previous plan. The FTA has been pressuring Chicago to improve system safety since a woman was set on fire in November on a Blue Line train.</p><p><b>In the works</b>: The CTA said its increased enforcement since December is having a positive impact, with assaults on transit workers down 25% in January, and 29% in February. The agency also pointed to violent crime decreasing 19% this year through the end of February, compared to the same period in 2025. The Chicago Sun-Times reported last week that violent attacks on the CTA reached historic highs last year.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/03/10/chicago-transit-authority-cta-crime-security-plan-fta" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h3>ELECTIONS 🗳️</h3><div class="Enhancement" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtljdil-tlkjhhnv-k/" target="_blank" >Mayor says push for ‘democracy zones’ at polling places is lasting legacy of Jesse Jackson</a></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtljdil-tlkjhhnv-u/" target="_blank" >Fran Spielman</a></h5><p><b>Protecting voters</b>: Mayor Brandon Johnson’s proposed ordinance would establish "democracy zones" 100 feet beyond the perimeter that already prohibits electioneering around polling places. Federal immigration agents would be barred from entering those zones, and it also would be illegal to "intentionally publish personally identifiable information when done to cause harm or facilitate violence or stalking."</p><p><b>Federal 'provocations'</b>: In an email to City Council members last week, the mayor’s office argued elections officials across the country are "facing harassment, stalking and threats" and that the feds have "threatened to nationalize elections and deploy armed forces near polling places."</p><p><b>Honoring an icon</b>: The mayor is calling the measure the "Rev. Jesse L. Jackson Sr. Fair Access to Democracy Ordinance," which Johnson seems to hope will make it difficult for alderpersons to oppose the proposal.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/10/mayor-brandon-johnson-democracy-zones-polling-places-jesse-jackson" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h3>THE WATCHDOGS 🏛️</h3><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-390000" name="image-390000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a02a1bb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4599x2581+0+163/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5d%2F9a%2Ffe195aa04649bed3bd89b76f8a46%2Fmerlin-92874132.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7540a0b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4599x2581+0+163/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5d%2F9a%2Ffe195aa04649bed3bd89b76f8a46%2Fmerlin-92874132.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f48e29d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4599x2581+0+163/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5d%2F9a%2Ffe195aa04649bed3bd89b76f8a46%2Fmerlin-92874132.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="ASHLAND, VA - href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtljdil-tlkjhhnv-x/" target="_blank" >VA drops appeal in Supreme Court case, gives 1.2 million more vets extra education benefits</a></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtljdil-tlkjhhnv-m/" target="_blank" >Stephanie Zimmermann</a></h5><p><b>Appeal ends</b>: The U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs has withdrawn from a court battle related to the landmark U.S. Supreme Court decision in favor of Army veteran James Rudisill, creating a path for 1.2 million veterans to access up to 12 additional months of college through their GI Bill benefits.</p><p><b>About the plaintiff</b>: Rudisill began his court fight in 2015, after he was denied the ability to fully use both his GI Bill plans. He argued the VA shortchanged him out of 12 months of payments — money he was counting on to attend Yale University’s divinity school. The Supreme Court ruled 7-2 in April 2024 that Rudisill should have been allowed to tap both plans for a maximum of 48 months, as Congress intended.</p><p><b>Dodging the question</b>: VA press secretary Pete Kasperowicz said in an email that "after the court decision, VA worked for months with both political and career VA staff to develop the training, policies and procedures needed to appropriately implement the ruling." One question left unanswered is whether the department will reimburse veterans who took out loans to pay for school while the court case dragged on.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/11/veterans-affairs-montgomery-post911-gibill-rudisill-supreme-court-appeal-army-vet" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h3>MORE NEWS YOU Costumes from the “Wicked” films are displayed in the fourth exhibition hall. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtljdil-tlkjhhnv-yu/" target="_blank" >From ‘Wicked’ to ‘Hamilton,’ Paul Tazewell’s award-winning costumes dazzle</a></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtljdil-tlkjhhnv-jl/" target="_blank" >Ambar Colón</a></h5><p>In Paul Tazewell’s Oscar-winning costumes for "Wicked," art meets science in the dark, mossy coils on Elphaba’s Emerald City dress. In dressing the characters of "Hamilton," Tazewell took inspiration from 18th-century coats and corsets and blended them with contemporary streetwear trends, to make the characters feel more accessible and relatable.</p><p>Chicagoans can see this work up close in the new exhibit, "Crafting Character: The Costumes of Paul Tazewell," running at the Griffin Museum of Science and Industry through Sept. 7. It consists of artifacts from his early life and costumes from movies and musicals he’s worked on, including "West Side Story,” "The Wiz Live!" and "Harriet."</p><p>The art show came together when museum team members became curious about Tazewell's designs. Last year, he walked away from the 2025 Oscars with the award for best costume for "Wicked," starring Ariana Grande as Glinda and Cynthia Erivo as Elphaba — and dresses the actors wore are on display.</p><p>"Crafting" also features the revolutionary costumes from "The Sleeping Beauty," tailored to withstand movement as dancers run and leap across the stage.</p><p>"[Tazewell] has this message, which is, what moves you emotionally is the key to unlocking your own creative potential," said Voula Saridakis, head curator at the museum.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/museums-zoos/2026/01/28/paul-tazewell-wicked-hamilton-costume-design-museum-science-and-industry-oscar" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h3>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h3><p><b>If you could commission public art in Chicago, what and where would it be?</b></p><p>Reply to this email (please include your first and last name). We may run your answer in a future newsletter or story.</p><p>Yesterday, we asked you: <u>What’s something you do that you believe keeps you young?</u></p><p>Here’s some of what you said…</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="BlockQuote" > <blockquote><p>“Laugh out loud frequently.”</p></blockquote> <div class="BlockQuote-attribution">— Franklin Eric Dickerson</div> </div> </div> </div> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="BlockQuote" > <blockquote><p>“Enjoy cartoons. Listen to ‘Sesame Street’ songs and other kid music.”</p></blockquote> <div class="BlockQuote-attribution">— Steve Mattingly</div> </div> </div> </div> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="BlockQuote" > <blockquote><p>“I have done Nia, a body-mind-spirit movement practice for over 20 years, and now that I’m in my late 60s, I feel it has kept me young-ish! ... Anything that helps you keep moving, find joy and keep energy for improving the world is important.”</p></blockquote> <div class="BlockQuote-attribution">— Nancy Matthews</div> </div> </div> </div> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="BlockQuote" > <blockquote><p>“I had my last two kids when I was [ages] 41 and 42! Kept me young!”</p></blockquote> <div class="BlockQuote-attribution">— Deborah Leininger</div> </div> </div> </div> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="BlockQuote" > <blockquote><p>“I’m 75 and being in contact with people of all ages keeps me in touch with the world.”</p></blockquote> <div class="BlockQuote-attribution">— Ellen Brady</div> </div> </div> </div> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="BlockQuote" > <blockquote><p>“Don’t stress about nothing!”</p></blockquote> <div class="BlockQuote-attribution">— Patricia Heard</div> </div> </div> </div> <hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by</b>: Esther Bergdahl<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you.</i></p><p><i>Become a member to make stories like these free and available to everyone. </i></p><p><i>Learn more at </i><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtljdil-tlkjhhnv-jj/" target="_blank" ><i>suntimes.com/member</i></a><i>.</i></p>
entries.author: Esther Bergdahl | WBEZ

entries.title: 2 killed after storms bring at least 4 twisters, possible record-setting hail in Illinois and Indiana
entries.summary: <p>Two people were killed and several others were injured after severe storms produced at least four possible tornadoes and potentially record-setting hail in Illinois and Indiana, officials said. </p><p>The deaths occurred in Lake Village, Indiana, but further information was not immediately available. </p><p>"It hit us hard," Lori Postma, a spokesperson for Indiana's Lake Township Fire Department, said at a press conference Wednesday morning. "We will be working feverishly to bring our little town back to where it once was."</p><p>One supercell resulted in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/weather/2026/03/10/widespread-flooding-likely-in-chicago-area-as-threat-of-severe-storms-brings-potential-hail-and-tornadoes" target="_blank" >at least four tornadoes</a> across Livingston and Kankakee counties in Illinois, and Newton, Jasper, and Starke counties in northwestern Indiana, the National Weather Service said.</p><p>The same storm system that was responsible for the tornadoes also produced exceptionally large and potentially record setting hail.</p><p>In Kankakee, hail between three to five inches of diameter fell, with one hailstone measuring at six inches and possibly setting an Illinois state record for the largest hailstone, according to the weather service.</p><p>Another supercell produced two to four inches of hail across the southern and western Chicago metro area, officials said. A hailstone measuring 4.8 inches in diameter was found in Darien, Illinois. </p><p>Around 7 p.m. Tuesday, a large tornado touched down in Newton County, Indiana and caused damage to several homes, Indiana officials said. </p><p>The Lake Township, Ind. Fire Department, along with several other agencies, were out until about 4 a.m. Wednesday conducting primary searches of all damaged areas, officials said. </p><p>About 12,000 Northern Indiana Public Service Company customers were impacted by the storms, which caused severe damage to parts of Newton County, Indiana's electrical system, where there were at least 70 damaged electrical poles and "hundreds of locations with downed wire," according to NIPSCO spokesperson Jessica Cantarelli.</p><p>Four Lake Township firefighters suffered damage to their homes, with one of the homes requiring demolition, Postma said. </p><p>Abbey Rossiter, a Newton County commissioner, highlighted the importance of prioritizing support for those impacted by the storms. </p><p>"Many of those [impacted] have suffered unimaginable loss and devastation," Rossiter said. </p><p>The North Newton School Corporation were working with the Red Cross to set up North Newton Jr./Sr. High School in Morroco as a temporary shelter. </p><p>The weather service will be sending survey teams to asses the storm damage Wednesday.</p><p>"We are a very strong community and we will be just fine," Postma said of Lake Village. <br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Maxx out, Trey in: Ravens agree to sign edge rusher Hendrickson
entries.summary: <p>The Ravens are out on Maxx Crosby and in on Trey Hendrickson.</p><p>Less than a day after <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/10/bears-maxx-crosby-ravens-raiders-say-back-out-trade-defensive-end-nfl" target="_blank" >trade deal for the Raiders star fell through</a> after he reportedly failed the Ravens' physical, the Ravens agreed to sign Hendrickson Wednesday morning. The former Bengals star will get $112 million over four years, per ESPN. </p><p>Signing Hendrickson will do nothing to dissuade pockets of the league from believing that the Ravens simply got cold feet about trading for Crosby, who is just two months removed from having knee surgery. Rather than give up two first-round picks for Crosby and his three-year, $106.5 million contract, the Ravens had to part with less money per year and no picks to land Hendrickson. </p><p>Hendrickson played only seven games last year but had a whopping 17 ½ sacks in each of the 2023 and 2024 seasons.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a>, of course, remain in the market for edge-rush help. They monitored the Crosby market the first time around and at one point were the gambling favorite to land him.</p><p>The Raiders seem unlikely to be able to fetch two first-round picks for Crosby now, given the amount of spending most teams around the league have done since the start of legal tampering Monday.</p><p>Time will tell whether the Raiders look to move Crosby again or try to fix their soured relationship with their once and future star.</p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Tornado touches down near Kankakee as storms bring hail, continue to threaten flooding in Chicago
entries.summary: <p>A tornado touched down near Kankakee Tuesday night, tearing east from there and into Northwest Indiana, according to the National Weather Service.</p><p>Kankakee County Sheriff Mike Downey said no injuries or deaths had been reported yet, though first responders were still checking for injuries and assessing damage. His office said the tornado crossed the Kankakee River into Aroma Park where there was "extensive" damage.</p><p>The weather service said after touching down, the tornado moved along the far south side of Kankakee near the Kankakee Airport about 6:21 p.m. and had reached the community of Hopkins Park half an hour later. The tornado was later spotted and confirmed by radar as it was in Indiana, between Roselawn and Thayer, around 7:30 p.m., according to the weather service.</p><p>According to Commonwealth Edison, just over 7,000 customers in the area of the tornado were without power as of 10:30 p.m.</p><p>Kankakee County board chair Matthew Alexander Hildebrand and Kankakee mayor Chris Curtis filed an emergency declaration with the state, according to a statement released by Downey.</p><p>The sheriff warned that debris, including downed power lines and trees, were making travel difficult and urged people to avoid the roads if possible. He also discouraged any non-emergency calls to emergency services due to the phone lines being overwhelmed.</p><p>"I want to remind area residents to check on their neighbors and loved ones but to avoid unnecessary travel, if at all possible," Downey said in a <a class="Link" href="https://www.facebook.com/photo/?fbid=1380225434147463&set=a.222501509919867" target="_blank" >statement</a>. "I also want to remind everyone to be prepared for any additional storms that could potentially hit our area overnight."</p><p>Convoys of police vehicles from several local municipalities were rolling through the dark streets, illuminating cars and buildings searching for destruction and signs of people in distress.</p><p>Multiple mobile command centers from local fire departments, including Orland Park, were set up in a gas station parking lot on East Ridge Road and Route 57.</p><p>A pickup truck — its lights still on — was thrown through a detailing business south of Kankakee as downed power lines crossed the streets and Illinois State Police directed traffic nearby. </p><p>The tornado started in Livingston County and touched down intermittently before picking up near Kankakee, according to David King, a meteorologist with the weather service.</p><p>"It strengthened near Kankakee and became a pretty serious tornado before tracking east through Northwestern Indiana," said David King, a meteorologist with the weather service.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-540000" temperatures</a>. </p><p>Severe thunderstorms were still moving into Chicago from DuPage County around 8:30 p.m., King said, and severe thunderstorm warnings were extended until at least 1:30 a.m. Wednesday for Cook County. He said more lightning, wind gusts, hail and heavy rain could be expected through the night.</p><p>Widespread flooding throughout the Chicago area was also a possibility, and the weather agency warned commuters to avoid flooded streets.</p><p>"If you encounter any sort of floodwaters, never drive through," Kevin Doom, another meteorologist with the weather service, told the Sun-Times earlier Tuesday.
